title: A Rebuttal to Speech Soaked in Falsehood!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's rebuttal, chronology, donations, quoted reports, rhetorical questions, and paragraph order. The Tamil reading copy below restores the source-printed opening salutation and corrects clear OCR character and word-boundary errors encountered during translation. The canonical source document remains unchanged. Claims about the Assembly, official conduct, and contemporary reports remain attributed to the letter. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: During the Tamil Nadu Assembly debate on the Social Welfare Department's demand for grants on 15 July 2014, Dr Pushpaleela Alban spoke for the Kazhagam and listed achievements under the DMK government. She mentioned, among other measures, assistance of five lakh rupees provided to boys and adolescent girls who had lost their parents in the tsunami.

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa then intervened. She said that when the tsunami struck, she travelled repeatedly throughout Tamil Nadu to affected areas, provided many forms of assistance, and visited the injured in hospitals. She said that I was then the Assembly member for Chepauk but, despite being in Chennai, did not even go to Chepauk or meet the affected people of my constituency. When Kazhagam members attempted to explain, the Speaker said that no one could speak after the Chief Minister and refused permission. The Kazhagam members therefore walked out.

When the tsunami struck, was I sitting at home in good health and refusing to visit the affected areas? Had I not fallen ill two days before the disaster? Did the Chief Minister not know that? Newspapers reported that I was in hospital. Did she conceal that and falsely accuse me of remaining at home in Chennai without visiting the affected areas?

The tsunami struck Tamil Nadu on the morning of 26 December 2004. Two days earlier I had developed severe pain in my side. After treatment at home brought no relief, I was admitted to Apollo Hospital on the 26th. I am not revealing this now for the first time. The Kazhagam General Secretary, the Professor, announced it in a statement then, and it was reported in the newspapers.

Although I was in hospital and could not immediately visit the affected places, I sent M. K. Stalin, Arcot Veerasamy, T. R. Baalu and others to meet and console the people in Chennai. At around 10 a.m., I telephoned Dayanidhi Maran, then a Union minister, in Delhi and asked him to meet the Prime Minister and seek deployment of the armed forces to save those affected. From the hospital itself, on the 26th, I issued a statement calling for immediate relief work in the districts.

While I was in hospital, a false rumour about me spread and the house received numerous calls. I gave a television interview denying it. When Sonia Gandhi and Pranab Mukherjee came to Apollo Hospital to inquire about my health, I presented a cheque for one crore rupees from the Kazhagam towards tsunami relief.

Then Union Home Minister Shivraj Patil, Ambika Soni, Governor Surjit Singh Barnala, Tamil leader K. Veeramani, G. K. Vasan, Vaiko, R. Nallakannu, Thirunavukkarasar, C. P. Radhakrishnan, E. V. K. S. Elangovan, Kaviperarasu Vairamuthu, Justice Rathinavel Pandian, Professor Kader Mohideen, S. R. Balasubramaniam and Jayanthi Natarajan also visited Apollo Hospital and inquired about my health.

On 7 January 2005, Prime Minister Manmohan Singh, who had come to review the tsunami devastation, visited my home to inquire about my health. I gave him a cheque for twenty lakh rupees from the DMK Trust for tsunami relief. In addition, my remuneration of eleven lakh rupees for writing the screenplay and dialogue for *Mannin Maindhan*, and ten lakh rupees from *Kannamma*, a total of twenty-one lakh rupees, was sent through M. K. Stalin to then Chief Minister Jayalalithaa on 10 January 2005.
Only after recovering did I go to Anna Arivalayam on 11 January 2005 and resume Kazhagam work. Yet nine years later, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa faults me for not visiting the affected people.
Was it not reported at the time that when the tsunami occurred, Jayalalithaa was asleep and officials were unable to inform her, and that she learnt of it only around 10 a.m.?

How did the government machinery perform in tsunami relief? *Junior Vikatan* of 2 January 2005 published an article titled "Government Machinery that Would Not Move in an Emergency." *Kumudam Reporter* wrote under the title "Lethargy in Rescue Operations." The 1 January 2005 issue of *Nakkheeran* portrayed page after page of governmental inaction. On 29 December 2004, *The Hindu* published an editorial titled "A Poor Relief Effort."

Am I generally someone who hesitates to meet affected people? During MGR's rule, a great cyclone turned the Thanjavur region into a floodplain. I was then in prison. The moment I was released, without even going home to rest, I travelled directly from prison to the cyclone-hit Thanjavur district and consoled the people.
When a fire at a wedding hall in Tiruchirappalli burned 54 people to death, I went there and offered consolation even though I was not Chief Minister.
When 94 children were consumed by fire at a Kumbakonam school in July 2004, I went there in person and consoled the families.
At a major ceremony in Nagapattinam on 31 January 2007, I inaugurated houses constructed for tsunami survivors by the Mata Amritanandamayi Math.

Had my health been sound, I would have visited the tsunami-affected areas immediately. Let me say more. When a police officer informed me at 5 a.m. that an accident might occur at Puzhal Lake, I went to the Secretariat at that early hour. Because its doors had not yet been opened, I waited outside until the key arrived. At 6 a.m., I proceeded with officials to Puzhal Lake itself.

It is therefore improper for a person holding the office of Chief Minister to falsely say that I remained in Chennai and did not go. Newspapers reported that I was in hospital, and Sonia Gandhi, Pranab Mukherjee and many others visited me there. I have presented all these details only to establish that the accusation made against me by the Chief Minister in the Assembly was incorrect.

Why, even Chief Minister Jayalalithaa cancelled the anti-terrorism pledge-taking event at the Secretariat on 21 May, Rajiv Gandhi's memorial day, and did not attend it. It had been announced that on Gandhi's birthday, 2 October, the Governor and Chief Minister would garland Gandhi's statue on Kamarajar Salai in Chennai. Jayalalithaa did not appear even at the last moment. Finally, it was said that she had a toothache. Illness is a fact of nature. It is wrong to conceal it and level accusations.

On another occasion, when unprecedented rain and floods caused people to suffer across Chennai and several districts, Jayalalithaa was in Hyderabad. Some newspapers reported that she was conducting a ritual there.
At that time, former President of India K. R. Narayanan died. While everyone issued messages of condolence, Jayalalithaa did not even issue one.

In December 2005, when Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister and heavy rain caused flooding, I left at about 12.30 p.m. on Sunday, 4 December, toured the affected areas of Kotturpuram, Adyar and Saidapet, and consoled the people. Only after hearing this news did Chief Minister Jayalalithaa leave Poes Garden at 3.15 p.m. to visit the flooded areas.

One could continue trading examples like this endlessly. But it is not proper for a Chief Minister speaking in the Assembly to make the false claim that I stayed in Chennai and did not visit the tsunami-affected areas. When Kazhagam members attempted to reject that false accusation and state the truth, the Speaker denied them permission. Is that consistent with legislative convention? If no one may speak after the Chief Minister has spoken, is what takes place there democracy?
With affection,
M. K.
18 July 2014
